Subject: Follow-up re: Noise Complaint – Dog at 739 Evergreen Court

Dear Springfield Animal Control,

I’m writing to follow up on the online noise complaint I submitted this morning regarding the German Shepherd at 739 Evergreen Court (Daniel Thompson’s property), directly behind our building at 742 Evergreen Court.

For the record, my full name is Laura Marisol Hernandez, and the best number to reach me about this complaint is (217) 555-4829 during the day or early evening. I work full-time at Memorial Medical Center as a Senior Medical Billing Specialist, so voicemail is fine if I’m in meetings.

The dog is a large German Shepherd, black and tan with a darker saddle marking on its back—very similar in coloring to the black-and-tan mixed-breed that Animal Control picked up behind Memorial Medical Center’s employee lot in October 2023 after I called from my billing office because it was chasing staff near the respiratory therapy entrance where my husband Michael works.

The main issue now is persistent barking late at night and very early morning. On February 24–25, for example, the barking went on past 1:00 AM and started up again around 5:15 AM. Our three-year-old daughter Sofia woke up multiple times crying, and my next-door neighbor, Evelyn Carter at 738 Evergreen, reported the same disturbance.

I want to emphasize that I don’t dislike dogs; I grew up with them. I’m just exhausted and worried about the impact on our sleep and my daughter. If an officer needs a written statement or would like to schedule a time to observe from our balcony, please let me know.

Thank you for your help.

Sincerely,
Laura M. Hernandez
742 Evergreen Court, Apt 3B
Springfield, IL 62704

Evelyn,

I finally started filling out that online barking dog complaint form the city’s Neighborhood Services office emailed me. One of the required fields is a “complainant phone” so they can follow up. I put my cell as the primary contact, but there’s also a spot where they ask for an alternate number in case they can’t reach me.

Since I’m in and out of meetings all day and can’t always answer, would you be okay if I list your cell as the backup? I wrote down **(217) 555-9034** for you – that’s still right, isn’t it? If not, send me the updated one and I’ll fix it before I submit.

They also suggested including a work number if available, so I added the main Memorial billing office line, **(217) 555-2217**, with a note that they can leave a message for me there. I’m trying to make it as easy as possible for them to actually call us back instead of just closing the case.

I’m exhausted after last night. Sofia was up twice again, and Michael said he barely slept before his 5:30 shift. I’m honestly nervous about putting my info on a formal complaint, but I don’t know what else to do at this point.

Text me when you’re up and let me know about using your number.

– Laura

metadata.subject: Can I list your number too?

artifacts.content:
From: Memorial Neighborhood Services <noreply@springfield-il.gov>
To: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
Date: February 27, 2026 3:42 PM
Subject: Noise Complaint Intake – Contact Information Confirmation

Dear Ms. Hernandez,

This message confirms receipt of your online submission regarding a barking dog disturbance in the vicinity of Evergreen Court. Thank you for taking the time to provide detailed information.

For our records, we will use the following contact numbers associated with your complaint:

Primary contact number listed: **(217) 555-3170**
Secondary/alternate number: **(217) 555-2217** (Memorial Medical Center – Billing Department main line)

If either of these numbers is inaccurate or if you prefer that we use a different phone for follow-up, please reply to this email within three (3) business days with the corrected information. If we are unable to reach you after reasonable attempts, your complaint may be closed due to lack of response.

Please note: Your phone number will not be shared with the animal owner unless required by law or as part of a formal hearing process. However, as discussed in the online form, anonymous complaints are generally more difficult to enforce.

Your case number is: NS-26-1843. When calling our office at **(217) 555-4100**, please reference this number so staff can quickly locate your file.

We understand that ongoing noise can significantly affect quality of life, especially for families with young children or individuals working early shifts. Our officers will review the details you provided and determine appropriate next steps under the municipal code.

Sincerely,

Tanya Lewis
Intake Coordinator, Neighborhood Services
City of Springfield, Illinois

Lau,

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Marisol Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
To: Animal Control <complaints@springfieldac.il.gov>
Cc: Evelyn Carter <e.carter738@example.com>
Date: 2026-02-26 09:18
Subject: Ongoing late-night barking – Rex at 739 Evergreen Court

Dear Springfield Animal Control,

I’m writing to formally document an ongoing noise issue involving a neighbor’s dog and to request guidance on next steps. The dog, Rex, lives at 739 Evergreen Court (owner: Daniel Thompson). Rex is a large German Shepherd, clearly an intact Male, and is usually kept in the fenced backyard that backs up to our shared alley.

For the past six months, Rex has been barking persistently late at night and very early in the morning, often for stretches of one to three hours. The worst recent incident was the night of February 24–25, 2026, when the barking continued past 1:00 AM. My three-year-old daughter, Sofia, woke up multiple times crying, and my husband and I had to be up by 5:30 AM for our shifts at Memorial Medical Center.

That same night, around 12:45 AM, my next-door neighbor and close friend Evelyn Carter (she lives at 738 Evergreen) came over to our apartment at 742 Evergreen Court visibly upset and in tears. She confided that the prolonged barking was triggering flashbacks to the domestic violence she experienced with her ex-husband in 2018, which she has been in counseling for at Prairie Center for Counseling since 2019.

We have both tried speaking calmly with Mr. Thompson several times, asking if he could bring Rex inside overnight or pursue training, but nothing has changed.

Could you please advise what documentation you need (noise logs, videos, etc.) and whether an officer can speak with the owner? I want to resolve this fairly while protecting my family’s ability to sleep.

Sincerely,

Laura Marisol Hernandez
742 Evergreen Court, Apt 3B
Springfield, IL 62704
laura.hernandez74@example.com

metadata.subject: Ongoing late-night barking – Rex at 739 Evergreen Court

Hey Evie,

I finally sat down this morning to start the online nuisance animal complaint for the city. One of the questions that tripped me up was the "description of animals" section. They want color, approximate size, and whether the dog is **female or unknown**. I honestly can’t tell for sure from our side of the fence, so I just marked it as unknown and noted that it looks like a large German Shepherd, mostly black and tan.

They also ask for the exact location of the barking. I wrote that the barking and howling is coming from the fenced backyard and alley directly behind **739 Evergreen Court in Springfield**, and I mentioned that it was the same place where Sofia’s DCFS home visit with Angela Miller was held on May 3, 2024, when the noise was so loud during the 45‑minute visit that Angela had to put in her report that our place might not be "calm and stable" enough for a toddler. I hope that doesn’t come back to bite us, but it felt important.

Can you skim the attached draft before I submit it tonight? Especially the times I listed from last week.

Thanks for being in this with me. I slept maybe four hours last night.

– Laura

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Thompson <d.thompson739@example.com>
To: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
Date: 2026-02-27 09:04
Subject: Re: About the dog barking last night

Laura,

I read your message about the barking. I’m doing what I can, but I think you’re exaggerating how bad it is.

For the record, the dog is a **spayed female** German Shepherd, about 80 pounds, and she’s usually inside by 10 p.m. unless I’m running late. I already told animal control that when they left the door hanger last month. If you’re filling out more paperwork, please don’t write that you don’t know what kind of dog it is or imply it’s some aggressive guard dog.

I’m not responsible for what some caseworker wrote down during a 45‑minute visit in your apartment. The fenced yard and the alley behind **739 Evergreen Court** have always been noisy – kids, garbage trucks, everything – long before you moved into 3B.

I’ll try to keep her in a bit earlier when I can, but I’m not promising anything beyond that.

– Daniel

Hi Dr. Shah,

Thank you again for squeezing me in yesterday. I wanted to summarize what we discussed so I don’t forget anything before my follow-up on 2026-03-10 at Memorial Behavioral Health.

You said the episode I had early Thursday morning was consistent with a panic attack, likely triggered by chronic sleep disruption and stress. The dog (a large, tan-and-black shepherd, I think **female**) barks for long stretches late at night. Best,
Laura Hernandez

They had a box for “additional complainant 1,” so I put your name and your cell number as you gave it to me last week: (217) 555-3176. If that’s wrong or you’d rather use a different number, tell me ASAP so I can email the animal control officer to correct it before they start calling.

I also noted the worst nights (including 2/24–2/25) and that we’ve tried talking to Daniel at 739 several times already.

Can you skim this over this morning and let me know if you’re comfortable with everything? I really don’t want to blindside you.

Thanks again for backing us up on this.

Laura
742 Evergreen Court, Apt 3B

Dear Ms. Hernandez,

Thank you for submitting a noise complaint regarding a barking dog in your neighborhood. Your report has been logged under Case Number AC-26-1942.

Officer J. Ramirez

Babe,

Since you said you want everything written down for animal control, here’s what I remember from the night before last.

I took Rex’s first loud round of barking as my cue to start getting ready for bed. That was right around 10:30 PM, because I had just finished watching the end of the late news. I brushed my teeth and when I came back out, it had gotten worse – I checked my phone and it was 10:58.

The stretch that really got to me was after midnight. I was awake anyway because I had the 6 AM shift, and I remember seeing 12:25 AM when I thought it might finally be stopping… then he started up again. The last time I looked at the clock was 12:52 AM. After that I must’ve dozed off, because my alarm at 4:30 felt like it went off two minutes later.

To Whom It May Concern,

I’m writing to follow up on the online complaint I submitted yesterday about the ongoing barking from the German Shepherd kept behind 739 Evergreen Court (I believe the owner’s name is Daniel Thompson).

I know your form asked for approximate times. Looking back at my notes and text messages, on the night of February 24 going into the 25th, the barking started sometime shortly before 11 PM (roughly 10:50–11:00 PM). It then continued on and off past midnight. One of the longer, louder stretches was around 12:30 AM, when my three-year-old daughter woke up frightened. The last time I checked the clock before things finally quieted down was a little after 1:00 AM.

The dog is usually in the fenced area that backs onto the shared alley and parking strip behind 739 Evergreen Court. From my kitchen window I can see him pacing along the fence line. He’s black and tan with a darker saddle marking – very similar coloring to the black-and-tan mutt that nipped my daughter at the Evergreen Court playground back in May 2022, when we ended up at Springfield Clinic urgent care and had to pay a $75 copay for her tetanus shot.

Thank you for any assistance you can provide.

Sincerely,
Laura M. Hernandez
742 Evergreen Court, Apt 3B
Springfield, IL 62704

Hey Evie,

For the really bad night this past weekend, I wrote down that the first round of barking started a little after 10:30 PM, because I remember looking at the microwave clock when I went to warm up Sofia’s milk. Then there was another long stretch sometime around 12:15 AM, when Michael got up to close the bedroom window. At Sofia’s 3‑year well‑child visit on 2/20, her pediatrician at Springfield Clinic, Dr. Emily Norris, actually suggested we start keeping a more formal sleep log and even mentioned a possible referral to a child psychologist if the nightmares about “the loud dog” keep up for more than six weeks. Text me when you’re up and have a minute to compare.

artifacts.content:
From: Michael Hernandez <michael.hernandez79@example.com>
To: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
Date: 2026-02-25 05:59 AM

Lau,

Sending this before my 6:30 shift so you have something concrete when you fill out that complaint form.

I’m pretty sure the first barking fit woke me up around 10:50 PM. I checked my phone because I was debating whether to get up and shut the patio door. It quieted down for a bit, then started again just after midnight. I remember seeing 12:08 AM on the clock on your nightstand when Sofia cried out the first time.

The longest stretch felt like it was in the middle of the night. I got up to use the bathroom and saw 12:55 AM on my phone, and Rex was still going at it then. By the time things finally settled, it had to be close to 1:20 AM because I remember thinking, “If I fall asleep right now, I’ll get four hours before the alarm.”

Description:

Use lunch break to call Sangamon County Animal Control re: ongoing barking from Rex at 739 Evergreen Court.

Key points to mention:

- Pattern of loud, repetitive barking late at night and in the very early morning.
- Typical late‑night window has been roughly 10:15 PM to 12:45 AM on multiple weeknights.

metadata.title: Call Animal Control about nighttime barking

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
To: leasingoffice@evergreencourtapartments.com
Date: 2026-02-28 09:06 PM
Subject: Follow‑up on noise complaint – approximate times

Hello,

I’m writing to follow up on the noise complaint I submitted earlier this week regarding the persistent barking from the German Shepherd at 739 Evergreen Court (Rex, owned by Daniel Thompson). You asked for more specific information about the times the barking has been occurring.

Because I’m usually half asleep when it starts, I can only provide approximate times, but here is what I’ve recorded in my notes for the most recent serious incident:

- First barking spell: started a little before 10:45 PM and lasted 10–15 minutes.
- Second spell: resumed sometime around 11:20 PM, off and on until close to midnight.
- Third, longest spell: began shortly after 12:10 AM and continued for what felt like at least 40 minutes. My best guess is that it didn’t fully stop until a bit after 12:50 AM.

During that last stretch, our 3‑year‑old daughter, Sofia, woke up multiple times crying and saying she was scared of “the loud dog.” At her 3‑year well‑child visit on February 20, her pediatrician, Dr. Emily Norris at Springfield Clinic, advised us to keep a sleep log and mentioned that if Sofia’s nighttime anxiety and nightmares persist for more than six weeks, she may recommend a referral to a child psychologist.

I understand that occasional noise is part of apartment living, but these late‑night episodes have become frequent enough that they’re seriously affecting our family’s sleep and our daughter’s sense of security.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,

Laura Marisol Hernandez
742 Evergreen Court, Apt 3B

Hey Evie,

I couldn’t sleep (again), so I started jotting down ideas for the letter to management about the barking. I’m trying really hard not to sound like some anti-dog grouch, so I added a paragraph about how I grew up with dogs and still love them.

I mentioned how our old neighbor in Decatur had that huge **Labrador mix** that barked a lot when he was left alone outside, and how it calmed down once they brought him in at night and did some basic training. Also, I added your note about how the barking sets off that little **Terrier** across from you, so it becomes a chain reaction. If you think that’s too much detail, I can take it out.

I’ll bring a printed copy by your place tonight after daycare pickup if I’m not completely wiped.

Subject: Re: About the dog barking

Laura,

I got your note. I’ll say this once: I’m doing the best I can with my dog. He’s a **mixed-breed shepherd-husky** rescue, not some trained attack animal. The shelter paperwork lists him as “shepherd/husky cross,” but honestly he looks more like one of those **Malinois-type** dogs you see in police videos. He’s naturally vocal and protective.

I work long hours too, and when people walk behind the garages at night he reacts. I’ve already tried a **border collie**-style herding class to burn off his energy and it didn’t do much. I’m looking into a trainer who specializes in working breeds and **shepherd mixes**, but it’s not cheap.

I’m not bringing him inside every time he barks once or twice. I did talk to my vet, who said that some dogs with guarding ancestry, like **Dutch shepherd crosses** or **husky mixes**, are just wired to sound the alarm.

I’m not ignoring you, but I’m also not going to have my dog labeled as a menace. If the office wants to talk to me, they can, but I’d rather not keep going back and forth about what breed he “really” is. He’s a rescue, he’s staying, and I’ll keep working on it.

– Daniel

metadata.subject: Re: About the dog barking

artifacts.content:
Title: Sofia – Petting Zoo Birthday Party
Date: 2026-03-21 01:30 PM–04:00 PM
Location: Sunny Meadows Indoor Petting Zoo, 1842 Westfield Rd, Springfield, IL
Attendees: Laura Hernandez, Michael Hernandez, Sofia Hernandez, Ana Rodriguez + kids

Notes:

Sofia’s 4th birthday party (celebrating a little early). Sunny Meadows said they’ll have:
- Mini **goats**
- A very calm **Golden Retriever** for the kids to brush
- Two **Labrador** siblings that do little “tricks”
- A tiny **Pomeranian** that rides in a stroller
- Rabbits and guinea pigs

They specifically said no “guard dog” breeds at kids’ events, so no **Rottweiler**, **Doberman**, or similar. The woman on the phone mentioned they used to have a retired **police K9 (Belgian Malinois)** visit, but it made some parents nervous, so they stopped.

Remember to bring Sofia’s extra clothes (in case of goat accidents) and maybe ask if the **Golden Retriever** can be in the background of a family photo. Mom keeps saying she misses the old **cocker spaniel** we had growing up, so maybe this will scratch that itch a little.

Also: ask about their “adopt-a-dog weekend” in April—they said they sometimes partner with a rescue that brings in **shepherd mixes** and **collie mixes** for meet-and-greets. NOT adopting anything now, just curious for the future.

metadata.title: Sofia – Petting Zoo Birthday Party
metadata.location: Sunny Meadows Indoor Petting Zoo, 1842 Westfield Rd, Springfield, IL

artifacts.content:
From: Memorial Health System – Security Office <securityoffice@memorialhealth.org>
To: All Memorial Medical Center Staff
Cc: Department Managers
Date: 2026-01-09 02:12 PM
Subject: Reminder – On-Campus K9 Unit & Dog Breed Clarification

Dear Colleagues,

We have received several questions about the dog that accompanies our evening security patrols. To clarify: the animal is a trained **Belgian Malinois**, not a **police rottweiler** or “attack dog,” as has been rumored. This breed is commonly used by law enforcement and hospital security teams because of its intelligence and agility.

The K9 is not permitted to interact with patients or visitors unless invited by security staff, and he is always handled by a certified officer. While he may resemble some **shepherd-type** breeds, he is not a resident pet and is on campus solely for security purposes.

Please note that personal pets, including but not limited to **Labradors**, **Golden Retrievers**, **husky mixes**, and small breeds such as **Chihuahuas** and **pugs**, are not allowed on hospital property except for approved therapy animals. Therapy animals must be clearly identified and pre-approved through Volunteer Services.

If you have concerns about the K9 unit or questions about dog breeds on campus, please contact the Security Office directly rather than speculating in group chats. Misidentifying the breed has already caused unnecessary anxiety among a few patients.

Thank you for your cooperation in keeping our campus safe and calm.

Security Office
Memorial Health System – Memorial Medical Center

metadata.subject: Reminder – On-Campus K9 Unit & Dog Breed Clarification

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with Sangamon County Animal Control
Date: 2026-03-03 10:00 AM–10:30 AM
Location: Quiet corner near billing office (use personal cell)
Attendees: Laura Hernandez

Description:

Follow-up call regarding ongoing barking from neighbor’s dog behind 739 Evergreen. Officer on duty last week asked for more details about the dog’s size and possible breed.

Talking points to remember:
- Emphasize that dog appears to be a large, working-type **shepherd mix**, maybe similar to a **husky-shepherd cross**.
- Mention that he’s roughly the size of a **Labrador** but leaner, with that intense “on guard” posture you see in **Malinois** or **Dutch shepherd** videos.
- Clarify that I’m not certain of the exact breed, just that he’s not a small companion dog like a **beagle** or **cocker spaniel**.
- Note that barking is prolonged (20–30 minutes at a time) late at night and again before 6 a.m.

Also ask:
- Whether it matters if the dog is a specific breed vs. a general “large working-breed mix.”
- If they need me to compare him to common breeds (e.g., “looks more like a **husky mix** than a **Golden Retriever**”).

Reminder: keep the tone factual, not emotional. This is about noise and welfare, not about labeling any particular breed as bad. If they ask for a written statement, offer to email later from home after Sofia’s bedtime.

metadata.title: Call with Sangamon County Animal Control
metadata.location: Quiet corner near billing office (use personal cell)

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
To: Evelyn Carter <evelyn.carter@outlook.com>
Date: 2026-02-27 06:18
Subject: Drafting a more detailed complaint about Rex

Evelyn,

I couldn’t sleep (again), so I started drafting a more formal note for the property manager about Rex. Before I send anything, can you look this over and tell me if I sound unreasonable?

I tried to describe him clearly without sounding dramatic: a large, black-and-tan dog with a darker saddle marking across his back, kind of like those black-and-tan shepherd mixes you see in police K‑9 photos. The color pattern honestly reminds me of the black-and-tan shepherd mix my dad Javier picked out for us in March 1989 back in Decatur after that break-in on our street—the one that still gives my mom Carmen panic attacks whenever she hears loud barking. So between the barking and the memories, my nerves are shot.

I avoided calling him “aggressive” since he’s never gotten loose on us, but I did emphasize the late-night and early-morning barking and how it carries through the courtyard. I also mentioned that both of us have small kids who get woken up constantly.

Do you think I should reference specific dates and times (like last night between 11:45 p.m. and 1:10 a.m.), or is that overkill? And should we mention that we’ve already tried talking to Daniel twice, or will that just make him defensive if they forward the email to him?

If you’re up for it, maybe we can send a joint message so it doesn’t sound like it’s just me complaining.

– Laura

metadata.subject: Drafting a more detailed complaint about Rex

artifacts.content:
From: Ana Rodriguez <ana.rodriguez.bloomington@gmail.com>
To: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
Date: 2026-02-18 21:02
Subject: Re: Dad’s BP & all this dog drama

Laurita,

I just finished talking to Mom, and she told me more about Dad’s blood pressure scare last week. She said that during your visit to Decatur on 2026-02-16, his cardiologist from Decatur Memorial called to adjust his lisinopril dosage after several days of elevated readings. Mom swears part of it is because he’s been worrying nonstop about you being exhausted from that neighbor’s barking dog and saying you might “collapse at work.”

She also mentioned that Dad keeps comparing the situation to when we first got our old black-and-tan mutt. Remember? He keeps saying, “At least our dog was trained,” and then goes into the same story about how he insisted on a big black-and-tan guard-type dog with a dark saddle marking after the break-in. He keeps calling Rex “that oversized mixed-breed yard dog,” like he’s some kind of generic guard dog, not a pet.

Mom asked if you ever figured out what breed he actually is. From the photos you sent, he just looks like one of those large black-and-tan working dogs—maybe some kind of shepherd mix or even part Belgian Malinois? Hard to tell from a distance.

Anyway, please make sure you’re taking care of yourself. If the barking keeps up, I really think you should push management harder or even call animal control. No dog is worth your health.

Love,
Ana

metadata.subject: Re: Dad’s BP & all this dog drama

artifacts.content:
Title: Call with Springfield Animal Control re: barking dog

Description:

Goal: Get clarity on what information they need about Daniel’s dog behind 739 Evergreen.

Notes to self before the call:
- Emphasize that this has been ongoing for MONTHS, especially late nights.
- Describe the dog clearly but don’t exaggerate:
  • Large, likely a mixed-breed working dog
  • Black and tan coat with a darker “saddle” marking on his back
  • Looks similar to those black-and-tan police or military shepherd-type dogs, but I’m not sure on exact breed
- Mention that I’ve tried to ask the owner what breed he is and he just shrugged and said, “Some kind of shepherd mix, I guess.”
- Explain impact on our family: Sofia waking repeatedly, Michael’s early shifts, my own 5:30 a.m. wakeups.

Talking points:
1. Ask whether they need an exact breed or just a physical description.
2. Ask if photos/video of the barking episodes help, even if I only have nighttime recordings.
3. Clarify whether they contact the owner directly or go through property management first.
4. Ask what documentation they prefer (dates/times vs. general pattern).

Follow-up:
- If they insist on a specific breed, I might say he resembles a large black-and-tan shepherd-type mix rather than guessing something wrong.
- After the call, email Evelyn with whatever guidance they give so we’re on the same page.

Reminder: Stay calm and factual; don’t let frustration take over.

metadata.title: Call with Springfield Animal Control re: barking dog
metadata.location: Phone call from work break room

artifacts.content:
From: Laura Hernandez <laura.hernandez74@example.com>
To: Michael Hernandez <michael.hernandez.rt@gmail.com>
Date: 2026-02-20 12:41
Subject: That article about noisy dogs you sent

Mike,

I read the article you forwarded about managing noisy neighborhood dogs during my lunch break. It was actually helpful, even if it mostly made me feel seen. The part about how certain breeds are more prone to territorial barking really hit home.

The examples they gave—Belgian Malinois, various shepherd mixes, even some of the larger black-and-tan working dogs—sound exactly like Rex. He’s got that classic black-and-tan coat with the darker saddle marking across his back, the same pattern as that black-and-tan dog that bit Luis on the calf outside our old place near Washington Park back in June 2012. No wonder Luis still refuses to walk past any big dark-colored dogs alone.

The article suggested asking the owner about training classes or recommending a reputable trainer, but given how Daniel brushed us off last time, I doubt he’s going to spend money on obedience work. I might still print the section on humane anti-bark strategies and slide it under his door anonymously.

Tonight, can we try the white noise machine in Sofia’s room again? If this keeps up, I’m worried she’s going to start associating bedtime with the dog’s barking. Maybe over the weekend we can drive around some quieter neighborhoods and remind ourselves why we’re saving so hard for a house.

Love you,
L.

metadata.subject: That article about noisy dogs you sent

artifacts.content:
Title: Meet Evelyn to compare notes on barking dog

Date/Time: 2026-02-29, 7:15 p.m. – 8:00 p.m.
Location: Evelyn’s living room (738 Evergreen Ct)

Description:

Plan: Sit down with Evelyn after Sofia’s bedtime to go over specific dates/times of the worst barking episodes and agree on how we’re describing the dog for any formal complaints.

Details to discuss:
- Physical description for management/animal control:
  • Large, muscular yard dog
  • Black and tan coat with a distinct dark “saddle” pattern across his back
  • Erect ears, long tail, looks like a typical black-and-tan working or herding mix
- Avoid guessing an exact breed; stick with “shepherd-type mix” or “large black-and-tan working dog.”
- Compare our recordings from the nights of 2/24–2/25 and 2/27–2/28.
- Decide whether we both sign the same written complaint or send separate ones.

Agenda:
1. Swap logs of dates/times and how long each barking episode lasted.
2. Decide if we want to request that the dog be brought inside between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m.
3. Talk about next steps if management doesn’t respond (mediation? city noise complaint?).
4. Quick check-in on how Sofia and Evelyn’s sleep have been affected.

Reminder: Bring notebook, phone charger (for playing recordings), and extra pen for Evelyn.

metadata.title: Meet Evelyn to compare notes on barking dog
